18: et tikvat chut hashani hazeh tiksheri bevad haChalon: 'this scarlet thread [tikvat chut hashani] you shall tie in the window.' Tikvah can mean both 'cord/thread' and 'hope' — some patristic interpreters (Justin Martyr, Origen) noted that the word for Rahab's hope (tikvah) is the same as the word for her scarlet cord; her hope and the scarlet cord are linguistically the same thing. Whether or not this wordplay is original, the typological point is clear: the scarlet cord marks the household for salvation as the Passover blood marked the doorposts; those inside are safe, those outside are not. Rahab's inclusion in Matthew's genealogy (Matt 1:5) is a Gentile woman's faith being woven into the Messiah's line.
1: vayyishlach Yehoshua ben-Nun min-hashittim shnayim anashim meraggelim chashah — "Joshua sent two men secretly as spies." Meraggelim (spies) comes from ragal (to walk about, to spy out); the earlier spying mission in Numbers 13-14 had catastrophic results from twelve men. Two men, sent secretly, parallels the earlier two faithful spies (Caleb and Joshua). Zonah — the word for Rahab's profession can mean either prostitute or innkeeper in some ANE contexts; the LXX uses pornē (prostitute), Heb 11:31 and Jas 2:25 follow this, and the context implies the same.
2: The king of Jericho's intelligence network is immediately active: the spies are known to have entered the city. The verb lun (to lodge/spend the night) indicates the spies stayed overnight — heightening the urgency of discovery. The rapid information flow reveals a city on high alert, aware that Israel has camped across the Jordan.
3: hotsi'i et ha'anashim haba'im elayikh asher ba'u leveitakh — "bring out the men who came to you, who entered your house." The king's phrasing treats Rahab as complicit by virtue of hospitality — her house is the site of the security breach. Lasur et kol ha'arets ba'u — "for they have come to spy out all the land" — the same infinitive construct used of the earlier Mosaic spying mission.
4: vatiqach ha'ishah et shnei ha'anashim vatitspenehu — "the woman took the two men and hid them." The perfect vatiqach and vatitspenehu describe her quick action before the king's messengers arrived. Her lie to the king (lo yada'ati me'ayin hema — "I did not know where they were from") is the text's ethical crux: she deceives the king to protect the spies. The narrator presents her action as faith-driven without commenting on the ethics of the deception.
5: Rahab's misdirection — the men left at dusk when the city gate was closing, pursue them quickly — is precisely timed and plausible. Bishca'or lisgor hashaar vahachoshekh — as darkness and gate-closing coincide, the window for pursuit seems narrow. Her detailed false testimony serves the spies' safety. The Talmud later debated whether her lie was justified by the greater good of Israel's mission.
6: vehi he'eletatam hagagah vatitmenehem bepishte ha'ets — "she had taken them up to the roof and hidden them under the stalks of flax she had laid out." Pishta (flax) — stalks of harvested flax laid out to dry on the flat roof — was common in ancient Canaan. The detail is realistic: the roof was a work space. The flax concealment is a practical detail that also carries symbolic weight: flax was used to make linen, which is associated with priestly garments.
7: The king's men pursue the spies toward the Jordan fords; hama'abarot (the fords) are the crossing points on the Jordan — the same topographic feature Joshua will exploit in chapters 3-4. The city gate shuts behind the pursuers, sealing the spies' escape route — but also, unknowingly, completing the city's own isolation before judgment.
8: veheimah terem yishkavun vehi al'ta alehem al hagag — "before they lay down, she came up to them on the roof." The timing — before the spies settle for sleep — shows Rahab's urgency. She knows that what she is about to say will determine her fate and her family's. Her initiative frames the following speech as her own act of faith, not merely a diplomatic negotiation.
9: yada'ti ki natan YHWH lakhem et ha'arets — "I know that the LORD has given you the land." Her confession uses the Hebrew perfect natan — already given, from God's side, before the conquest begins. Napal ematkhem aleinu — "your terror has fallen on us" — emah (terror/dread) is a specifically covenantal terror: YHWH promised to send divine dread before Israel (Exod 23:27; Deut 2:25). Rahab recognizes this terror as divine action.
10: shama'nu et asher hovish YHWH et mei yam-suph — "we heard how the LORD dried up the Red Sea." The Exodus events (forty years prior) are still the defining news of the region. Hekharam (devoted them to destruction) — the same cherem root that governs Jericho's treatment. Rahab's knowledge is secondhand but sufficient: hearing produces faith, the same pattern Paul describes in Rom 10:17.
11: vayimmas levavenu velo qama od ruach be'ish mippeneikhem — "our hearts melted and no spirit remained in anyone because of you." Mamas = to melt, dissolve (the same word for the wax-melting of Ps 68:2). ki YHWH elohekhem hu elohim bashamayim mima'al — "for the LORD your God, he is God in heaven above and on earth below." This is a Gentile woman's monotheistic confession — structurally identical to the Shema's implications — drawn from observing YHWH's acts in history rather than from receiving the Torah.
12: weatta hishavi'u na li beYHWH — "now swear to me by the LORD." Rahab invokes YHWH's name for the oath — not Baal or any Canaanite deity. Her appeal is to the one she has just confessed as the God of heaven and earth. va'asitem gam atem im beit avi chesed — "and you will also show chesed (faithful love) with my father's household." She uses the covenant term chesed — the steadfast love that characterizes YHWH's dealings with his people — in her negotiation. A Canaanite prostitute appropriates Israel's covenant vocabulary.
13: vehichyitem et avi ve'et immi — "and you will spare my father and my mother." Hichyitem (hiphil of chayah) = cause to live, preserve alive. The scope of the request — father, mother, brothers, sisters, and all who belong to them — mirrors the Passover pattern: those inside the marked household are protected; the community of the rescued is defined by the sign (the scarlet cord/blood on the doorposts).
14: naphshenu tachteikhem lamut — "our life for yours to die." The idiom pledges the spies' lives as surety for Rahab's. Im lo taggidu et davarenu zeh vehayah ka'asher yitaneha YHWH lanu et ha'arets — when YHWH gives us the land, we will deal faithfully with you. The oath is conditional: Rahab must not disclose their mission. Chesed we'emet — the same paired virtues that describe YHWH's character (Exod 34:6); the spies promise YHWH-like faithfulness.
15: vatoridem becheevel derech hachalon — "she let them down by a rope through the window." The rope is the physical connection between Rahab's world and Israel's. ki veitah beqir hachomah uveqir hachomah hi yoshevet — "her house was in the city wall and she lived in the wall." A house built into the city wall itself: her position is architecturally exposed, yet the most secure location for the escape — the wall is also the place of greatest danger when the city falls, but the scarlet cord marks her window for protection.
16: Three days in the hill country — the same three-day period that appears throughout Joshua's early chapters (cf. 1:11; 3:2). Pen tipge'u bam — "lest you meet them" — the pursuit is real and ongoing. The instruction to hide until pursuit ceases is tactically precise. The three-day structure has liturgical resonance: three days of hiddenness followed by emergence/deliverance.
17: neqiyim anachnu mishvu'ateikh hazeh — "we will be free of this oath you have bound us to." The men qualify the oath before departing: the scarlet cord must be in the window at the time of Israel's arrival, and the family must be inside the house. If Rahab fails to maintain these conditions, the oath is voided. The precise legal language of the oath mirrors Israelite covenant structure: conditions, signs, and consequences.
19: vekhol asher yetse midaltei veitakh hachutsa damo beroshon — "blood will be on his head." The idiom of blood-guilt (damo beroshon) is standard legal Hebrew for self-incurred responsibility: those who leave the marked house during the attack die by their own choice. The inverse is equally precise: those inside are under the oath's protection regardless of their personal standing. The house, not personal merit, is the determining factor.
20: The oath-release condition — if Rahab reveals the mission — uses the same legal language as the previous verse. Neqiyim anachnu mishvu'ateikh = free from your oath. The careful delineation of conditions shows that this is a formal covenant arrangement, not merely a promise. Both parties have obligations; both parties bear consequences for breach.
21: vatiqshor et tikvat hashani bachalon — "she tied the scarlet cord in the window." Tikvah means both "cord/thread" and "hope" — Rahab's hope is the scarlet cord; the thing that marks her household for salvation is simultaneously the word for expectation. The wordplay was noted by patristic interpreters (Justin Martyr, Origen) as a type of faith. The cord goes up immediately — she does not wait to see if the promise will be honored.
22: The three days of hiding in the hill country while the pursuit searches the road and the Jordan fords — the classic military misdirection. Vayashovu haradephim veyvaqshu velo matse'u — "the pursuers searched but did not find." The passive-voice absence of finding suggests divine concealment rather than mere luck. The three-day period mirrors the three-day notice given to Israel before the Jordan crossing.
23: vayashuvu sheney ha'anashim vayyerdu min hehur vaya'avoru — "the two men returned, came down from the hills, crossed over." The return journey is reported tersely: three verbs, no difficulty. The spies' mission succeeded; they carry intelligence and a covenant. They report to Joshua vet kol ha'arets — all that had happened to them.
24: vayyomeru el Yehoshua ki natan YHWH veyadenu et kol ha'arets — "the LORD has given all the land into our hands." The spies' report is pure faith-affirmation, using the same prophetic perfect the spies from Numbers 13 failed to speak: not "this is the land; the inhabitants are fearsome" but "YHWH has given it." Gam namagu kol yoshevei ha'arets mippaneinu — "all the inhabitants of the land have melted before us" — namagu = they have dissolved. Rahab's testimony (2:11) is confirmed as the state of the entire land.
